evidence_summary: Dossier reports a React Router article component surface at `app/articles/content` serving `/articles/{category}/{slug}`.
`app/routes/articles.index.tsx` is a listing candidate for `/articles` and imports `getArticlesSortedNewestFirst` and `resolveArticleRouteSlug` from `~/articles/registry`.
`app/routes/articles.slug.tsx` is a detail candidate and imports `getArticleBySlug`, `ArticleLayout`, and uses lazy/glob discovery of article content modules.
Candidate metadata links `app/routes/articles.index.tsx` and `app/routes/articles.slug.tsx` through `app/routes.ts` with content path pattern `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx`.
Existing article TSX files are shown under multiple `app/articles/content/...` category directories.
`app/routes/resources.tsx` redirects to `/articles`, which reinforces `/articles` as the canonical listing surface.
The dossier's legacy detector also found `app/articles/content` as an article-system candidate.
article_surface_resolution.why_this_is_article_system: The strongest evidence is the React Router article pair: `app/routes/articles.index.tsx` serves `/articles` and imports article registry functions such as `getArticlesSortedNewestFirst`, while `app/routes/articles.slug.tsx` serves article detail routes and imports `getArticleBySlug`, `ArticleLayout`, and lazily discovers article content modules. The dossier explicitly reports a React Router article component surface at `app/articles/content` serving `/articles/{category}/{slug}`. Because `app/articles/content` is not available as a selectable candidate, `app/articles` is the closest provided canonical content-source locator for the article registry/content system.
why_other_candidates_lost: `app/articles/content/featured` and `app/articles/content/careers` contain article TSX files, but they are category subsets rather than the whole canonical article system root.
`app/articles/content/featured/page.tsx` is a listing-like page under a content category route, not the main `/articles` listing/detail route pair.
`app/routes/resources.tsx` only redirects to `/articles`, so it supports `/articles` being canonical rather than being canonical itself.
`app/routes/esafety.resources.tsx`, `app/routes/hospital.app.resources.tsx`, and `app/routes/watt-the-hack.resources.tsx` are scoped resources routes, not the repository-wide article system.
`app/routes/valley.update.new.tsx` is only listing-like by route naming and has no convincing connection to the article registry/detail system.
`app/components/articles` contains reusable article UI components, not stored article entries or a public route surface.
`app/components`, `app/components/hospital`, `app/components/hackathon`, and `app/components/logo-shooter` are component directories without evidence of being the canonical article content system.
`app/articles/resources.ts` is a low-confidence static/support file and not the main listing or detail surface.

No publish mutation target was selected because the safest apparent article-entry root is `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx`, but the root `app/articles/content` is not one of the provided publish candidates; choosing a single category such as `featured` or `careers` would be unnecessarily specific.

The primary resolution is supported by strong route and code evidence: `app/routes/articles.index.tsx` is the `/articles` listing surface importing article registry helpers, `app/routes/articles.slug.tsx` is the detail surface importing `getArticleBySlug` and `ArticleLayout`, and the dossier links both through `app/routes.ts` to TSX article modules under `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx`. `app/routes/resources.tsx` redirecting to `/articles` further supports `/articles` as canonical. The selected content source `app/articles` is slightly broad because the actual entries appear under `app/articles/content`, but that root was not available as a candidate and `app/articles` is the closest provided parent/registry locator, so this imprecision does not justify downgrading the article surface itself.

evidence_summary: The repository is detected as a React Router 7 TypeScript/Vite app with route roots under app/routes.

The listing candidate app/routes/articles.index.tsx has react_router_component_articles evidence for listing route /articles and imports getArticlesSortedNewestFirst and resolveArticleRouteSlug from ~/articles/registry.
The detail candidate app/routes/articles.slug.tsx has matching react_router_component_articles evidence for /articles/* and imports getArticleBySlug from ~/articles/registry.
The detail surface snippet describes automatic discovery of article content modules using Vite glob imports.
Candidate metadata connects listing app/routes/articles.index.tsx, detail app/routes/articles.slug.tsx, router file app/routes.ts, and content path pattern app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx.
The dossier and legacy detector report existing TSX article files under app/articles/content, including multiple category/slug examples.
The app/articles candidate contains article registry/support files including app/articles/registry.ts, authorRegistry.ts, authors.ts, and resources.ts.

Manual follow-up is still warranted because the exact detected content root app/articles/content is not itself available as a canonical candidate, so app/articles is selected as the conservative candidate-level root.
article_surface_resolution.why_this_is_article_system: The strongest connected article surface is the React Router /articles system: app/routes/articles.index.tsx is the public listing, app/routes/articles.slug.tsx is the detail surface, both are tied to ~/articles/registry, and the dossier identifies TSX article modules under app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx. Because the exact content root app/articles/content is not provided as a selectable candidate, app/articles is the safest candidate-level canonical content source and hook mutation target: it contains the registry/support modules and encloses the detected article content tree.
why_other_candidates_lost: app/articles/content/featured and app/articles/content/careers contain real TSX article files, but they are category-specific subsets rather than the canonical whole article system.
app/components/articles contains reusable article UI components, not article records or the public route surface.
app/components and the other component subdirectories are broad or domain-specific component trees without evidence that new article entries are stored there.
app/routes/resources.tsx is only a resource route candidate and its sample redirects to /articles, so it is not the canonical listing itself.

app/articles/content/featured/page.tsx looks like a listing-like page, but it is inside the content tree, has a next_app_router route-source signal in a React Router repo, and is not the connected canonical /articles route.
app/articles/resources.ts is a support/listing data file candidate, not a public listing surface.
The esafety, hospital, watt-the-hack, and valley resource/update routes are narrower public surfaces and are not tied to the app/articles registry plus article detail route.

runContext.design_guide:
# MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 (current codebase adaptation) — Scope: branding, tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the current MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, marketing/page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** app. Observed package versions include: ```txt react: 19.2.1 react-router: 7.10.0 vite: 7.3.1 typescript: 5.9.3 tailwindcss: 4.1.17 ``` Use: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Do **not** use: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do **not** use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue/Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content files should export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`**. The route handler/layout layer already handles article layout concerns. - Do not add duplicate article title/header/breadcrumb/author sections if the route/layout renders them. - Body content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If using a wrapper, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article design-system components instead of raw ad-hoc card/callout/image markup. - Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, the built-in fallback behaviour of supported image components, or explicit placeholders like `` only in templates. - Ensure registry slug path and SEO config path match exactly. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` in generated article content unless explicitly requested; article layout concerns are handled outside the body content. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The codebase uses a mix of MLAI token variables and bold brutalist article colours. | Token / colour | Value / usage | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | observed in components | Bright teal surface/accent. Used in `ArticleStepList` bullets and brand borders. | | `--brand-ink` | observed in links | Teal link/hover colour on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | observed in components | Charcoal/dark text on bright brand surfaces. | | `--soft` | observed in components | Soft teal panel background. | | `--brutalist-orange` | CSS variable | Article/search filter orange. | | `--brutalist-purple` | CSS variable | Article/search filter purple. | | `--brutalist-black` | CSS variable | Article/search filter black. | | `--brutalist-yellow` | CSS variable | Article/search filter yellow. | | `--brutalist-beige` | CSS variable | Standalone page hero beige variant. | | `#ff3d00` | orange | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#4b1bd1` | purple | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#ffe900` | yellow | Brutalist yellow surface with black text. | | `#2b0f70` | deep purple | Resource CTA background. | | `#0d9488` | cyan/teal | Resource CTA background. | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use these existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Link emphasis on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article wrappers. - Invent new colour variants for existing components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` on article design-system components. - Overuse bright colours in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utility classes and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup The current Tailwind config is minimal and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography'; import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss'; export default { content: ["./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config; ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components. ### 4.2 Import convention Use the configured alias: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when matching an existing nearby file pattern is clearly necessary. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`. Use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use the existing article design-system components below. Do not create raw card grids, raw quote boxes, or raw image blocks when a component exists. ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code persona cards with raw `
` border/shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotes. Import: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'purple' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx AI adoption works best when the team starts with a clear workflow, a measurable outcome, and a review loop for quality and risk. ``` Do not use non-existent variants such as: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`, not raw HTML image markup. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` `ArticleImageBlock` uses the project’s `ImageWithFallback` internally and falls back to `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE`. Do not use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.4 FAQ Use `ArticleFAQ`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` Example: ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'How should a small team start with AI?', answer: 'Start with one workflow, define the outcome, and review outputs before scaling.', }, { question: 'Should every AI task be automated?', answer: 'Not necessarily. High-impact or high-risk decisions should keep a human review step, especially where accuracy, privacy, or customer trust matters.', }, ] ``` `ArticleFAQ` renders its own heading. Do not add another duplicate FAQ heading immediately before it. `ArticleFAQ` supports these item keys: ```ts export type ArticleFAQItem = { q?: string question?: string a?: ReactNode answer?: ReactNode } ``` Prefer `question` and `answer` for new content. Keep FAQ answers simple inline text or inline React nodes because the component renders the answer inside a paragraph. --- ### 6.5 Step lists Use `ArticleStepList`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'teal' | 'brand' | 'indigo' ``` For generated content, use `accent="teal"` unless explicitly matching an existing article pattern. Do not use unsupported variant props. ```tsx ``` Step items may be strings or `{ label: string }` objects. Prefer strings for simple generated content. --- ### 6.6 Table of contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` only when the article enhancer/layout is expected to populate it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Optional no-margin use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.7 References Use `ArticleReferences`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` Supported reference categories: ```tsx 'government' | 'guide' | 'analysis' | 'industry' | 'watchlist' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleReferencesProps = { references: ReferenceItem[] heading?: string description?: string headingId?: string previewCount?: number } ``` --- ### 6.8 Resource lists Use `ArticleResourceList`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Supported badge tones map to the internal badge variants: ```tsx 'red' | 'purple' | 'black' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx const resources: ArticleResource[] = [ { label: 'AI adoption checklist', description: 'A practical checklist for scoping, testing, and reviewing AI use cases.', href: '/articles', ctaLabel: 'Open', type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Free', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Do not reference missing components: ```tsx ``` These are not available in the current context. --- ### 6.9 Article CTA blocks #### Simple company CTA Import: ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts export interface ArticleCompanyCTAProps { title: string body: string buttonText: string buttonHref: string note?: string id?: string className?: string } ``` Keep CTA text generic. Do not hard-code specific product names unless instructed. #### Resource CTA Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'purple' | 'cyan' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.10 Related article cards Use `ArticleCardBySlug` when linking to another registered article. Import: ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'default' | 'compact' ``` The component resolves registered article metadata and route slugs internally. Supported props: ```ts type Props = { slug: string ctaText?: string className?: string descriptionOverride?: string variant?: 'default' | 'compact' authorOverride?: ArticleAuthor } ``` --- ### 6.11 Footer article nav Use `ArticleFooterNav` for end-of-article navigation if the article pattern requires it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleFooterNavProps = { backHref?: string backLabel?: string topHref?: string className?: string } ``` Do not add if the route/layout already provides equivalent footer navigation. --- ### 6.12 Author bio Use `AuthorBio` only if the article pattern requires an in-body author section and the layout does not already render one. Import: ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Most article content files should not manually render a hero/header. The route/layout handles article framing. Do not add: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' ``` Do not wrap with: ```tsx ... ``` Do not add `ArticleHeroHeader` in generated article body files unless explicitly requested for a bespoke page surface; it renders header concerns that are usually handled by the layout. ### 7.2 Standalone page hero For non-article standalone pages, `HeroSection` exists. Import: ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'yellow' | 'beige' | 'white' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` For generated article content, avoid using this unless the task explicitly asks for a full standalone page. --- ## 8) Component imports — approved quick reference Use these exact imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Only import what the file uses. --- ## 9) Article file one-shot skeleton Use this structure for a new article content module: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best first step?', answer: 'Start with a narrow workflow, a clear success measure, and a human review process.', }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI information', publisher: 'Department of Industry, Science and Resources', category: 'government', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

Start with a concise introduction that explains the practical problem, who the guide is for, and what the reader will be able to do next.

Start with the workflow

AI decisions are easier when the team starts from the workflow instead of the tool. Map the current process, identify the bottleneck, and decide what a useful improvement would look like.

Treat AI as a workflow capability, not a magic feature. The useful question is: what decision, draft, search, summary, or review step can become clearer or faster? ) } ``` Remove unused imports before final output. If the article uses a `Link`, import it exactly like this: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` --- ## 10) Metadata, registry, and SEO consistency When adding or updating an article: - Content file path should follow: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` - Route slug should match the registry entry. - SEO config path should match the registry slug path exactly. - Internal article links should use the final public route: ```tsx ... ``` - Use kebab-case for filenames and slugs. - Use Australian English. - Date-sensitive claims should be qualified, for example: “as at 2026”. Do not invent registry or SEO export names. Follow the existing structures in: ```txt app/articles/registry app/articles/seo-config ``` --- ## 11) Accessibility checklist Apply to every generated UI/content change: - [ ] Headings follow semantic order. - [ ] Do not duplicate layout-rendered article titles or author sections. - [ ] Link text is descriptive. - [ ] External links use `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"`. - [ ] Images use meaningful `alt` text unless decorative. - [ ] No placeholder image URLs. - [ ] Colour contrast is appropriate: - yellow uses black text - purple/orange use white text - light/transparent panels use gray/black text - [ ] Interactive elements have visible focus styles. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Lists use semantic `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` markup. --- ## 12) Copy and content rules Use the MLAI tone: - Australian English. - Practical, clear, and educational. - Define acronyms once. - Avoid hype, guarantees, and exaggerated claims. - Avoid implying official endorsement by government, universities, vendors, accelerators, or investors unless explicitly true. - When discussing AI, explain limits, review needs, data/privacy concerns, and verification steps in plain language. - When discussing startups or funding, keep the content educational and avoid financial advice language. - Do not add medical disclaimers. - Do not add legal, technical, or “Important” disclaimer blocks unless explicitly requested. - Do not add domain-specific service chips or “browse services by need” tag groups. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations” - “Explore resources” - “Read the guide” - “Plan your next step” - “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}” --- ## 13) Patterns and recipes ### 13.1 Internal article cross-link ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' how to get started with AI ``` ### 13.2 External source link ```tsx Australian Government AI resources ``` ### 13.3 Persona section ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Strong callout ```tsx If the workflow requires judgement, accountability, or customer trust, keep a human review step. ``` ### 13.5 Image block ```tsx ``` ### 13.6 Step-by-step section ```tsx ``` ### 13.7 References section ```tsx ``` ### 13.8 Generic CTA section ```tsx ``` ### 13.9 Resource CTA section ```tsx ``` --- ## 14) Do / Don’t quick list Do: - Use `react-router` `Link` with `to`. - Use alias imports such as `~/components/articles/QuoteBlock`. - Use `AudienceGrid` for persona cards. - Use `QuoteBlock` for quotes/callouts. - Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. - Use `ArticleFAQ` for FAQs. - Use `ArticleReferences` for source lists. - Use `ArticleResourceList` for resource lists. - Use `ArticleResourceCTA` or `ArticleCompanyCTA` for generic CTAs. - Use `bg-transparent` for generated wrappers. - Keep article files as body content only. - Remove unused imports. Don’t: - Don’t import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Don’t use Next.js APIs or imports. - Don’t use raw `` for article images. - Don’t use `placehold.co`. - Don’t use raw bordered card grids for persona cards. - Don’t use duplicate article headers, breadcrumbs, author bios, or FAQ headings. - Don’t use unsupported component variants such as `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. - Don’t use `bg-white` in generated article wrappers. - Don’t reference missing components such as `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. - Don’t add medical disclaimers, legal disclaimers, technical disclaimers, or service-chip sections unless explicitly requested. --- ## 15) Build and validation notes This project uses Bun-first scripts and React Router typegen/build commands. Relevant scripts: ```txt bun run build bun run typecheck bun run check:article-internal-links ``` Before finalising generated code: - Confirm all imports exist. - Confirm every imported component is used. - Confirm every component prop matches the actual TypeScript type. - Confirm no Next.js imports or directives appear. - Confirm article route path, registry slug, and SEO config path are consistent. - Confirm JSX returns a single fragment or valid wrapper. - Confirm no unsupported variants are used. - Confirm no raw article image markup or placeholder image URLs are used. - Confirm generated article content does not import `ArticleLayout`. - If adding `useEffect` cleanup code outside article content, return a void cleanup function body, not an expression result.
runContext.company_context:
# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is a not-for-profit AI and machine learning community based in Australia. It brings together builders, learners, founders, researchers, students, clinicians, data scientists, AI developers, entrepreneurs, and industry supporters through events, hackathons, educational programs, community tools, and startup support initiatives.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial intelligence, machine learning, startup ecosystem, technology education/community  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation with events, educational programs, hackathons, and software-based founder/community tools  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia; broader relevance to “Australia and beyond” where AI community impact is discussed

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun, and impactful while fostering a vibrant Australian community passionate about AI and machine learning.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs and build a thriving AI ecosystem through meetups, hackathons, educational programs, practical community support, and meaningful professional connections.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, founders, students, clinicians, and practitioners.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world challenges, useful projects, and applied AI rather than abstract hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise members who help, volunteer, mentor, support founders, and share updates.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together people from different backgrounds — developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, healthcare professionals, students, sponsors, and mentors.
- **Transparency and momentum:** Encourage founders to build trust through consistent updates, clear asks, and visible progress.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use MLAI as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI and machine learning learners:** Students, early-career professionals, and curious beginners who want to understand AI, attend events, meet peers, and build confidence.
- **Builders and practitioners:** Data scientists, AI developers, software engineers, researchers, and technical contributors looking for practical challenges, hackathons, workshops, community support, and collaboration.
- **Startup founders and entrepreneurs:** Founders using AI or building technology companies who need pitch support, investor update habits, community feedback, introductions, and fundraising preparation.
- **Healthcare and medtech innovators:** Clinicians, doctors, nurses, pharmacists, healthcare professionals, students, data scientists, and AI builders interested in solving real healthcare bottlenecks through events such as MedHack.
- **Sponsors and ecosystem partners:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel intimidating, abstract, or hard to start with.
- Builders need real-world problems, collaborators, mentors, and structured opportunities to apply their skills.
- Founders often start raising money too late and do not build investor trust early enough.
- Early-stage founders struggle to communicate momentum, metrics, asks, risks, and updates clearly.
- Healthcare systems face bottlenecks around ageing populations, inpatient wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural care, and preventative medicine.
- Community members want simple ways to get involved, contribute meaningfully, and be recognised for helping.
- Sponsors need credible, values-aligned ways to support and reach the Australian AI ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, investors, founders, and technical peers.
- Build prototypes and solve real-world AI challenges through hackathons.
- Improve pitch clarity and communicate ideas effectively.
- Build investor relationships months before a raise.
- Turn community contribution into tangible perks such as coworking, events, mentoring, merch, and office hours.
- Support Australia’s AI and ML ecosystem through sponsorship, partnerships, and community involvement.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of MLAI’s offering as a community ecosystem made up of events, tools, hackathons, and contribution loops.

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   Meetups, workshops, social events, lightning talks, and educational gatherings across Australia for builders, learners, founders, and practitioners.

2. **Hackathons:**  
   Practical AI challenge events, including upcoming hackathons such as MedHack: Frontiers and the eSafety Hackathon. These events bring participants together to prototype solutions to real-world problems.

3. **MedHack: Frontiers:**  
   A healthcare AI hackathon focused on applying AI to pressing healthcare challenges. It brings clinicians, builders, data scientists, AI developers, mentors, judges, investors, and students together to prototype solutions across streams such as ageing populations, wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural healthcare, and preventative medicine.

4. **MLAI Vibe Raising:**  
   A founder tool that helps startups draft monthly investor updates from information the founder chooses to provide or optionally connect. It is designed to help founders build investor relationships before they raise by turning wins, asks, challenges, metrics, risks, and follow-ups into visible momentum.

5. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions and can be used for rewards such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, mentoring, merch, and other community perks.

6. **Founder education and resources:**  
   Guides and content such as “How to Pitch Your Big Idea” that help founders communicate complex ideas, use frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution, and close with clear calls to action.

7. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   Sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first, not just content-first:** MLAI is positioned around active participation, contribution, Slack involvement, volunteering, events, and practical support.
- **Practical AI focus:** Events and hackathons are tied to real-world challenges, not just theory or hype.
- **Cross-disciplinary collaboration:** MLAI brings together technical people, founders, students, researchers, clinicians, mentors, investors, and sponsors.
- **Founder support integrated into an AI community:** MLAI supports not only AI learning and building, but also startup pitching, investor updates, fundraising readiness, and founder visibility.
- **Contribution recognition through Roo Points:** MLAI has an internal system for recognising meaningful community contributions and turning them into practical perks.
- **Australian ecosystem focus:** The brand is explicitly focused on supporting Australia’s AI and ML community and making Australia a stronger home for AI entrepreneurship.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals — pick 1–2 per article, max:**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit community based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members” from diverse backgrounds including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals, and students.
- MLAI events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong community demand.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks, meetups, workshops, hackathons, and educational programs.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run in collaboration with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers uses Monash University Clayton Campus as a venue.
- MLAI positions itself as supporting Australia’s AI future and connecting sponsors with developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.
- MLAI Vibe Raising has privacy and terms pages covering Google OAuth, Gmail data use, connected sources, AI-assisted monthly founder updates, retention, deletion, and Limited Use commitments.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do not claim MLAI is a for-profit SaaS company; it is MLAI Aus Inc and is described as not-for-profit/non-profit.
- Do not claim Roo Points are money, wages, an exchange rate, or a guaranteed pathway to paid work. The site explicitly says they are internal community reward points with no cash value.
- Do not claim Vibe Raising provides financial, legal, tax, investment, medical, or professional advice.
- Do not claim AI-generated outputs from MLAI tools are always accurate; the terms state outputs may be inaccurate, incomplete, outdated, or misleading and must be reviewed.
- Do not claim MLAI sells personal information, Gmail data, Google user data, or connected-source data; the privacy policy says it does not.
- Do not imply Gmail or connected-data access is mandatory for Vibe Raising; connectors are optional.
- Do not invent sponsor names, award wins, funding numbers, partner lists, or member statistics beyond “over 10,000 members.”
- Do not claim participation in events, Roo Points, or volunteering guarantees jobs, team roles, event ownership, investor introductions, funding, or paid work.
- Do not overstate geographic reach beyond the stated Australian focus, though the site does mention “Australia and beyond” in relation to AI impact.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line

Choose one per article:

- “MLAI is a not-for-profit Australian community helping people learn, build, and connect around AI and machine learning.”
- “MLAI brings Australia’s AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, and partners together through events, hackathons, tools, and community programs.”
- “MLAI is an Australian AI and machine learning community focused on practical learning, real-world projects, and meaningful professional connections.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools, educational resources, and community contribution programs.”

### Primary CTAs

Use ONE per article, aligned to topic.

**For beginners/learners:**
> “Join an upcoming MLAI event to meet Australia’s AI and ML community and start learning with other builders.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Explore MLAI hackathons and build practical AI solutions with other developers, researchers, founders, and domain experts.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or relevant hackathon page such as `/medhack`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “Learn more about MedHack: Frontiers and how clinicians, builders, and AI practitioners are tackling real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “Use MLAI Vibe Raising to draft monthly investor updates and build investor relationships before you raise.”  
> Target: `/vibe-raising`

**For community members:**
> “Meet Roo, MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Connect with MLAI’s Australian AI community through events, hackathons, and educational programs.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`, depending on intent

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### URL Patterns or Pages to Link To

- Events: `https://mlai.au/events`
- Hackathons: `https://mlai.au/hackathons`
- MedHack: `https://mlai.au/medhack`
- Vibe Raising: `https://mlai.au/vibe-raising`
- Roo: `https://mlai.au/roo`
- Sponsors: `https://mlai.au/sponsors`
- Privacy policy for Vibe Raising / Google and Gmail data use: `https://mlai.au/privacy`
- Terms of service for Vibe Raising and connected-source use: `https://mlai.au/terms`
- Pitching guide: `https://mlai.au/how-to-pitch-your-idea`

### CTA Rules

- Use ONE CTA per article, either as a natural aside near the relevant section or as a final helpful next step.
- Match the CTA to the article’s topic and reader persona.
- Keep CTAs practical and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- For founder/fundraising articles, point to Vibe Raising or the pitching guide.
- For AI learning, community, and industry articles, point to events.
- For healthcare AI articles, point to MedHack.
- For sponsorship, ecosystem, or partnership articles, point to the sponsors page.
- For community participation and contribution articles, point to Roo.
- Mention privacy, optional connectors, and review requirements when discussing Vibe Raising in any data-sensitive context.

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use hard-sell language like “Buy now,” “Guarantee funding,” “Secure investors instantly,” or “Get paid through Roo Points.”
- Do not imply MLAI provides regulated professional advice.
- Do not push Vibe Raising as an automated replacement for founder judgement, investor relations, accounting, legal review, or financial advice.
- Do not direct every article to Vibe Raising; many MLAI readers are learners, builders, sponsors, or community members rather than founders.
- Do not promise event availability, acceptance, perks, mentoring, coworking, or rewards if capacity is limited.
- Do not imply joining Slack, attending an event, or earning Roo Points guarantees employment, sponsorship, investment, or leadership roles.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:**  
Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, accessible, optimistic, and slightly informal. MLAI’s tone is energetic and helpful without being overly corporate. It should feel like a capable community organiser speaking to founders, builders, learners, and partners.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over theoretical:** Focus on what people can build, learn, attend, draft, improve, or contribute.
- **Community-first:** Use language that invites participation and recognises contribution.
- **Accessible and encouraging:** Make AI feel approachable, especially for beginners and cross-disciplinary audiences.
- **Optimistic but grounded:** Celebrate AI’s potential while staying focused on real-world problems and responsible use.
- **Founder-aware:** For startup content, emphasise momentum, transparency, clear asks, investor trust, and starting early.
- **Technically accurate but not exclusionary:** Explain concepts clearly without dumbing them down or turning articles into jargon walls.

### Do’s

- Use plain-English explanations before technical depth.
- Use Australian context where relevant, especially for AI careers, events, community, startups, healthcare, and policy.
- Use community language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “members,” “mentors,” “contributors,” and “AI community.”
- Highlight practical formats: meetups, workshops, lightning talks, hackathons, Slack, founder updates, pitch nights, coworking, mentoring.
- When discussing pitching, use direct, useful frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution and clear calls to action.
- When discussing Vibe Raising, mention that founders can optionally connect Gmail or other sources and must review AI-generated outputs.
- When discussing Roo Points, describe them as internal community recognition or thank-you points, not money.
- Use clear CTAs that invite readers to attend, join, build, contribute, draft, learn, or partner.

### Don’ts

- Don’t sound like a generic enterprise AI vendor.
- Don’t overhype AI as a magic solution.
- Don’t present MLAI as a university, accelerator, recruitment agency, investment fund, or consulting firm unless future materials explicitly say so.
- Don’t imply MLAI owns or operates Monash University, MYMI, Google, Slack, Xero, Linear, Notion, or other third-party services.
- Don’t write in a patronising tone toward beginners.
- Don’t frame community contribution as unpaid labour-for-wages; Roo Points are recognition and perks, not compensation.
- Don’t make guarantees around fundraising, jobs, investment, AI accuracy, hackathon outcomes, or event acceptance.
- Don’t use excessive corporate buzzwords such as “synergistic transformation,” “AI revolution platform,” or “end-to-end disruption engine.”

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred for general articles, given the Australian focus. Preserve official names and quoted website wording where needed.
- **Preferred terms:**  
  AI, machine learning, AI community, Australian AI community, builders, learners, founders, contributors, mentors, hackathons, meetups, workshops, lightning talks, Roo, Roo Points, Vibe Raising, monthly investor updates, founder updates, meaningful contribution, community perks.
- **Avoided terms:**  
  Guaranteed funding, guaranteed job, cash value, wage, exchange rate, investment advice, legal advice, medical advice, professional advice, fully automated investor relations, instant investor access, replacement for review, AI certainty.

### Content Rules

- Position MLAI as a not-for-profit Australian AI and machine learning community, not as a generic SaaS vendor.
- Keep the core message practical: learn, build, connect, contribute, and solve real problems.
- Use the “over 10,000 members” proof point sparingly and accurately.
- For founder articles, emphasise building investor relationships before raising, monthly updates, transparency, metrics, asks, risks, and momentum.
- For healthcare AI articles, connect content to real healthcare bottlenecks and cross-disciplinary collaboration between clinicians and builders.
- For Roo-related articles, explain that Roo lives in Slack and helps manage contribution recognition, rewards, coworking, requests, and admin flows.
- For Vibe Raising articles, state that connected data sources such as Gmail are optional and that users choose what to provide or connect.
- Always include the review caveat for AI-assisted outputs when relevant.
- Avoid implying that MLAI’s tools, events, or community programs guarantee outcomes.
- Do not invent details about sponsorship tiers, sponsors, awards, leadership, founding year, funding, or event attendance numbers.
- Keep CTAs light, useful, and aligned to the reader’s likely next step.

org_config_snapshot.design_guide:
# MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 (current codebase adaptation) — Scope: branding, tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the current MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, marketing/page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** app. Observed package versions include: ```txt react: 19.2.1 react-router: 7.10.0 vite: 7.3.1 typescript: 5.9.3 tailwindcss: 4.1.17 ``` Use: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` Do **not** use: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do **not** use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue/Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content files should export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`**. The route handler/layout layer already handles article layout concerns. - Do not add duplicate article title/header/breadcrumb/author sections if the route/layout renders them. - Body content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If using a wrapper, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article design-system components instead of raw ad-hoc card/callout/image markup. - Do not use placeholder image services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, the built-in fallback behaviour of supported image components, or explicit placeholders like `` only in templates. - Ensure registry slug path and SEO config path match exactly. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` in generated article content unless explicitly requested; article layout concerns are handled outside the body content. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The codebase uses a mix of MLAI token variables and bold brutalist article colours. | Token / colour | Value / usage | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | observed in components | Bright teal surface/accent. Used in `ArticleStepList` bullets and brand borders. | | `--brand-ink` | observed in links | Teal link/hover colour on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | observed in components | Charcoal/dark text on bright brand surfaces. | | `--soft` | observed in components | Soft teal panel background. | | `--brutalist-orange` | CSS variable | Article/search filter orange. | | `--brutalist-purple` | CSS variable | Article/search filter purple. | | `--brutalist-black` | CSS variable | Article/search filter black. | | `--brutalist-yellow` | CSS variable | Article/search filter yellow. | | `--brutalist-beige` | CSS variable | Standalone page hero beige variant. | | `#ff3d00` | orange | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#4b1bd1` | purple | Primary brutalist article card/callout colour. | | `#ffe900` | yellow | Brutalist yellow surface with black text. | | `#2b0f70` | deep purple | Resource CTA background. | | `#0d9488` | cyan/teal | Resource CTA background. | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use these existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Link emphasis on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article wrappers. - Invent new colour variants for existing components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` on article design-system components. - Overuse bright colours in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utility classes and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup The current Tailwind config is minimal and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography'; import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss'; export default { content: ["./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}"], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [ typography, ], } satisfies Config; ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components. ### 4.2 Import convention Use the configured alias: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when matching an existing nearby file pattern is clearly necessary. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`. Use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use the existing article design-system components below. Do not create raw card grids, raw quote boxes, or raw image blocks when a component exists. ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code persona cards with raw `
` border/shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotes. Import: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Allowed variants only: ```tsx 'purple' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx AI adoption works best when the team starts with a clear workflow, a measurable outcome, and a review loop for quality and risk. ``` Do not use non-existent variants such as: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`, not raw HTML image markup. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` `ArticleImageBlock` uses the project’s `ImageWithFallback` internally and falls back to `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE`. Do not use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.4 FAQ Use `ArticleFAQ`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` Example: ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'How should a small team start with AI?', answer: 'Start with one workflow, define the outcome, and review outputs before scaling.', }, { question: 'Should every AI task be automated?', answer: 'Not necessarily. High-impact or high-risk decisions should keep a human review step, especially where accuracy, privacy, or customer trust matters.', }, ] ``` `ArticleFAQ` renders its own heading. Do not add another duplicate FAQ heading immediately before it. `ArticleFAQ` supports these item keys: ```ts export type ArticleFAQItem = { q?: string question?: string a?: ReactNode answer?: ReactNode } ``` Prefer `question` and `answer` for new content. Keep FAQ answers simple inline text or inline React nodes because the component renders the answer inside a paragraph. --- ### 6.5 Step lists Use `ArticleStepList`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'teal' | 'brand' | 'indigo' ``` For generated content, use `accent="teal"` unless explicitly matching an existing article pattern. Do not use unsupported variant props. ```tsx ``` Step items may be strings or `{ label: string }` objects. Prefer strings for simple generated content. --- ### 6.6 Table of contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` only when the article enhancer/layout is expected to populate it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Optional no-margin use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.7 References Use `ArticleReferences`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` Supported reference categories: ```tsx 'government' | 'guide' | 'analysis' | 'industry' | 'watchlist' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleReferencesProps = { references: ReferenceItem[] heading?: string description?: string headingId?: string previewCount?: number } ``` --- ### 6.8 Resource lists Use `ArticleResourceList`. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Supported badge tones map to the internal badge variants: ```tsx 'red' | 'purple' | 'black' | 'yellow' ``` Example: ```tsx const resources: ArticleResource[] = [ { label: 'AI adoption checklist', description: 'A practical checklist for scoping, testing, and reviewing AI use cases.', href: '/articles', ctaLabel: 'Open', type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Free', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Do not reference missing components: ```tsx ``` These are not available in the current context. --- ### 6.9 Article CTA blocks #### Simple company CTA Import: ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Props: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts export interface ArticleCompanyCTAProps { title: string body: string buttonText: string buttonHref: string note?: string id?: string className?: string } ``` Keep CTA text generic. Do not hard-code specific product names unless instructed. #### Resource CTA Import: ```tsx import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'purple' | 'cyan' | 'orange' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.10 Related article cards Use `ArticleCardBySlug` when linking to another registered article. Import: ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'default' | 'compact' ``` The component resolves registered article metadata and route slugs internally. Supported props: ```ts type Props = { slug: string ctaText?: string className?: string descriptionOverride?: string variant?: 'default' | 'compact' authorOverride?: ArticleAuthor } ``` --- ### 6.11 Footer article nav Use `ArticleFooterNav` for end-of-article navigation if the article pattern requires it. Import: ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleFooterNavProps = { backHref?: string backLabel?: string topHref?: string className?: string } ``` Do not add if the route/layout already provides equivalent footer navigation. --- ### 6.12 Author bio Use `AuthorBio` only if the article pattern requires an in-body author section and the layout does not already render one. Import: ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Most article content files should not manually render a hero/header. The route/layout handles article framing. Do not add: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' ``` Do not wrap with: ```tsx ... ``` Do not add `ArticleHeroHeader` in generated article body files unless explicitly requested for a bespoke page surface; it renders header concerns that are usually handled by the layout. ### 7.2 Standalone page hero For non-article standalone pages, `HeroSection` exists. Import: ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'yellow' | 'beige' | 'white' ``` Example: ```tsx ``` For generated article content, avoid using this unless the task explicitly asks for a full standalone page. --- ## 8) Component imports — approved quick reference Use these exact imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Only import what the file uses. --- ## 9) Article file one-shot skeleton Use this structure for a new article content module: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best first step?', answer: 'Start with a narrow workflow, a clear success measure, and a human review process.', }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI information', publisher: 'Department of Industry, Science and Resources', category: 'government', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

Start with a concise introduction that explains the practical problem, who the guide is for, and what the reader will be able to do next.

Start with the workflow

AI decisions are easier when the team starts from the workflow instead of the tool. Map the current process, identify the bottleneck, and decide what a useful improvement would look like.

Treat AI as a workflow capability, not a magic feature. The useful question is: what decision, draft, search, summary, or review step can become clearer or faster? ) } ``` Remove unused imports before final output. If the article uses a `Link`, import it exactly like this: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` --- ## 10) Metadata, registry, and SEO consistency When adding or updating an article: - Content file path should follow: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` - Route slug should match the registry entry. - SEO config path should match the registry slug path exactly. - Internal article links should use the final public route: ```tsx ... ``` - Use kebab-case for filenames and slugs. - Use Australian English. - Date-sensitive claims should be qualified, for example: “as at 2026”. Do not invent registry or SEO export names. Follow the existing structures in: ```txt app/articles/registry app/articles/seo-config ``` --- ## 11) Accessibility checklist Apply to every generated UI/content change: - [ ] Headings follow semantic order. - [ ] Do not duplicate layout-rendered article titles or author sections. - [ ] Link text is descriptive. - [ ] External links use `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"`. - [ ] Images use meaningful `alt` text unless decorative. - [ ] No placeholder image URLs. - [ ] Colour contrast is appropriate: - yellow uses black text - purple/orange use white text - light/transparent panels use gray/black text - [ ] Interactive elements have visible focus styles. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Lists use semantic `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` markup. --- ## 12) Copy and content rules Use the MLAI tone: - Australian English. - Practical, clear, and educational. - Define acronyms once. - Avoid hype, guarantees, and exaggerated claims. - Avoid implying official endorsement by government, universities, vendors, accelerators, or investors unless explicitly true. - When discussing AI, explain limits, review needs, data/privacy concerns, and verification steps in plain language. - When discussing startups or funding, keep the content educational and avoid financial advice language. - Do not add medical disclaimers. - Do not add legal, technical, or “Important” disclaimer blocks unless explicitly requested. - Do not add domain-specific service chips or “browse services by need” tag groups. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations” - “Explore resources” - “Read the guide” - “Plan your next step” - “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}” --- ## 13) Patterns and recipes ### 13.1 Internal article cross-link ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' how to get started with AI ``` ### 13.2 External source link ```tsx Australian Government AI resources ``` ### 13.3 Persona section ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Strong callout ```tsx If the workflow requires judgement, accountability, or customer trust, keep a human review step. ``` ### 13.5 Image block ```tsx ``` ### 13.6 Step-by-step section ```tsx ``` ### 13.7 References section ```tsx ``` ### 13.8 Generic CTA section ```tsx ``` ### 13.9 Resource CTA section ```tsx ``` --- ## 14) Do / Don’t quick list Do: - Use `react-router` `Link` with `to`. - Use alias imports such as `~/components/articles/QuoteBlock`. - Use `AudienceGrid` for persona cards. - Use `QuoteBlock` for quotes/callouts. - Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. - Use `ArticleFAQ` for FAQs. - Use `ArticleReferences` for source lists. - Use `ArticleResourceList` for resource lists. - Use `ArticleResourceCTA` or `ArticleCompanyCTA` for generic CTAs. - Use `bg-transparent` for generated wrappers. - Keep article files as body content only. - Remove unused imports. Don’t: - Don’t import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Don’t use Next.js APIs or imports. - Don’t use raw `` for article images. - Don’t use `placehold.co`. - Don’t use raw bordered card grids for persona cards. - Don’t use duplicate article headers, breadcrumbs, author bios, or FAQ headings. - Don’t use unsupported component variants such as `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. - Don’t use `bg-white` in generated article wrappers. - Don’t reference missing components such as `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. - Don’t add medical disclaimers, legal disclaimers, technical disclaimers, or service-chip sections unless explicitly requested. --- ## 15) Build and validation notes This project uses Bun-first scripts and React Router typegen/build commands. Relevant scripts: ```txt bun run build bun run typecheck bun run check:article-internal-links ``` Before finalising generated code: - Confirm all imports exist. - Confirm every imported component is used. - Confirm every component prop matches the actual TypeScript type. - Confirm no Next.js imports or directives appear. - Confirm article route path, registry slug, and SEO config path are consistent. - Confirm JSX returns a single fragment or valid wrapper. - Confirm no unsupported variants are used. - Confirm no raw article image markup or placeholder image URLs are used. - Confirm generated article content does not import `ArticleLayout`. - If adding `useEffect` cleanup code outside article content, return a void cleanup function body, not an expression result.

MLAI -- One-Shot React Router Article/Resource Generation Spec (TSX) -- v2025-12-15 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent as a "system" / "developer" prompt so the agent can generate MLAI article/resource content for the current `mlai-au` codebase in one shot. The output must compile in the React Router v7 + React 19.2.1 + TypeScript + Tailwind project without manual corrections. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Resources should be practical, inclusive, factual, Australian-context aware, and date-qualified where helpful. Avoid hype, guarantees, exaggerated claims, and unsupported statistics. Output format: `.tsx` article content module(s) for the existing React Router codebase. Framework: React Router v7.10.0, React 19.2.1, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS. Package/runtime: Bun-first project. Build/typecheck commands use `bun run typecheck` and `bun run build`. Do not generate a PDF. Do not use ReportLab. Do not use Next.js patterns. ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given a Title, Slug, Category, Description, Date, optional Hero Image, article body outline/sections, FAQs, references, and optional CTA requirements, generate a TypeScript React article content file that adheres 100% to the rules below. Primary file path: ```txt app/articles/content/{category-slug}/{article-slug}.tsx ``` The file must: - Use `.tsx` - Export article metadata constants only when consistent with nearby article content files or explicitly required - Default export a function component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { ... } ``` - Return article body content only - NOT import or wrap in `ArticleLayout` - NOT render a duplicate article hero/title/header if the route/layout already renders it - Use React Router links from `react-router` - Use actual codebase components from `~/components/articles/...` - Use Tailwind utility classes and project CSS variables already present in the codebase - Compile with TypeScript - Include only imports that are actually used 3-viewer/browser QA: article renders in local dev, production build, and deployed React Router route without hydration errors, broken imports, invalid props, duplicated headers, or missing registry/SEO path consistency. Deliverables: - `app/articles/content/{category-slug}/{article-slug}.tsx` -- primary article content module - Registry/SEO updates only if the existing repository pattern requires them: - `app/articles/registry.ts` - `app/articles/seo-config.ts` - Do not invent new shared components or unsupported props. ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: string, slug: string, categorySlug: string, description: string, datePublished: string, // ISO date, e.g. "2026-01-15" heroImage?: string, // use a real URL or "", never placehold.co heroImageAlt?: string, sections: Array<{ heading: string, body: string | JSXContentPlan, subsections?: Array<{ heading: string, body: string | JSXContentPlan }> }>, audienceCards?: Array<{ title: string, description: string, variant: "orange" | "purple" | "yellow" }>, quoteBlocks?: Array<{ title?: string, variant: "purple" | "orange", body: string }>, images?: Array<{ src: string, alt: string, caption?: string }>, faqs?: Array<{ question: string, answer: string }>, references?: Array<{ href: string, title: string, publisher: string, description?: string, category?: "government" | "guide" | "analysis" | "industry" | "watchlist" }>, cta?: { title: string, body: string, buttonText: string, buttonHref: string, note?: string } } ``` Example input schema for planning only: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: "How to learn AI in Australia", slug: "how-to-learn-ai-in-australia", categorySlug: "ai-learning-resources", description: "A practical guide to learning AI in Australia, including community, projects, and credible resources.", datePublished: "2026-01-15", heroImage: "", heroImageAlt: "People learning artificial intelligence together", audienceCards: [ { title: "Students", description: "People exploring AI, machine learning, and data skills for study or early career decisions.", variant: "orange" }, { title: "Career switchers", description: "Professionals who want a practical path into AI without starting from scratch.", variant: "purple" }, { title: "Founders and builders", description: "Startup-minded learners who want to apply AI to real products and workflows.", variant: "yellow" } ], cta: { title: "Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}", body: "Explore practical MLAI resources and community pathways related to this topic.", buttonText: "Browse related resources", buttonHref: "/articles" } } ``` ## 2) Framework, routing, and project configuration Framework detection is authoritative: - Framework: React Router v7.10.0 - Rendering library: React 19.2.1 - Language: TypeScript - Component extension: `.tsx` - Styling: Tailwind CSS + existing CSS variables - Build tool: Vite - Router imports: `react-router` - Link component: `...` - Import alias: `~/` maps to `app/` - Package manager/runtime: Bun Never use: - `next/link` - `next/image` - `next/dynamic` - `next/font` - `'use client'` - `'use server'` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - `generateStaticParams` - `export const metadata` - Vue or Svelte syntax - CSS modules or styled-components unless already present in the touched file Use these project conventions: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleCallout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleCallout' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleResourceList from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Import ordering: 1. React/type imports, only if actually used 2. React Router imports, only if internal links are used 3. Icon imports, only if actually used 4. Internal component imports 5. Article utilities/authors, only if used No semicolons in generated article content files. ## 3) File path and export rules Primary article content file: ```txt app/articles/content/{categorySlug}/{slug}.tsx ``` Example: ```txt app/articles/content/ai-learning-resources/how-to-learn-ai-in-australia.tsx ``` Required component export: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Content files must NOT do this: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' export default function ArticlePage() { return ( ... ) } ``` `ArticleLayout` is handled by the route/registry layer. Importing or wrapping with `ArticleLayout` in the content file is a build/design failure. Metadata constants may be used when consistent with nearby article files: ```tsx const TOPIC = '{{TOPIC}}' const TITLE = '...' const DESCRIPTION = '...' const SLUG = '...' const CATEGORY = '...' const DATE_PUBLISHED = '2026-01-15' const HERO_IMAGE = '' ``` Do not invent a new metadata API. If updating `app/articles/registry.ts` or `app/articles/seo-config.ts`, follow the exact existing object shape in those files. Path consistency is mandatory: - Registry slug/path must match the content file slug. - SEO config path must match the registry route path exactly. - Example route path: ```txt /articles/{slug} ``` - Example content path: ```txt app/articles/content/{categorySlug}/{slug}.tsx ``` ## 4) Header, title, hero, and layout rules No duplicate headers. If the route or `ArticleLayout` renders the title, breadcrumbs, author, date, hero, SEO header, or disclaimer, the content file must not render them again. Content files should begin with article body content, such as: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

...

...

... ) } ``` Only use `ArticleHeroHeader` when the specific existing route/content pattern explicitly uses a custom header and the file exports the required flag, for example: ```tsx export const useCustomHeader = true ``` If using `ArticleHeroHeader`, import it exactly: ```tsx import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '~/components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader' ``` Valid `ArticleHeroHeader` props: ```tsx ``` Valid `headerBgColor` values only: - `cyan` - `purple` - `blue` - `orange` Do not use `ArticleHeroHeader` unless the generated file is intentionally a custom-header article. ## 5) Styling and brand rules Styling approach: Tailwind utilities and existing project CSS variables. Use actual classes/colors found in the codebase: - `bg-transparent` - `border-gray-300` - `border-gray-400` - `text-gray-900` - `text-gray-800` - `text-gray-700` - `text-gray-600` - `text-gray-500` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - `bg-[--brand]` - `bg-[--soft]` - `bg-[#ff3d00]` - `bg-[#4b1bd1]` - `bg-[#ffe900]` - `bg-[#2b0f70]` - `bg-[#0d9488]` - `rounded-[22px]` - `rounded-[28px]` - `rounded-[32px]` - `rounded-2xl` - `rounded-3xl` - `my-8` - `my-12` - `mt-8` - `mt-10` - `mb-6` - `space-y-3` - `space-y-6` - `grid gap-4 sm:gap-6 md:grid-cols-3` Generated wrappers must use: ```tsx
...
``` Never use generated wrapper classes with `bg-white`. Existing imported components may contain their own internal backgrounds. Do not rewrite component internals. Do not use unsupported brand variants. ## 6) Design system component rules Use existing article components instead of raw HTML UI blocks. Do not invent missing components. These components are not available: - `ArticleResourceDownload` - `ArticleSummaryCard` ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. Import: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid props: ```tsx ``` Valid `AudienceGrid` variants only: - `orange` - `purple` - `yellow` Never use these `AudienceGrid` variants: - `blue` - `brand` - `teal` ### 6.2 Callouts / quotes / highlighted notes Use `QuoteBlock` for narrative callouts, highlighted takeaways, and quote-style blocks. NEVER: - Generate PDF code. - Use ReportLab. - Import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`. - Use `next/link`, `next/image`, `next/font`, or any Next.js API. - Add `'use client'` or `'use server'`. - Use `export const metadata`. - Use `getStaticProps`, `getServerSideProps`, or `generateStaticParams`. - Use `bg-white` on generated wrappers. - Use `placehold.co`. - Use raw HTML cards when `AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, or `ArticleImageBlock` applies. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` on `AudienceGrid` or `QuoteBlock`. - Invent components such as `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`; these are missing. - Add service chips or "Browse services by need" blocks. - Add medical/legal-style disclaimers unless explicitly requested by the input and appropriate to the article. - Duplicate article title, breadcrumb, author, date, or hero if the layout renders them. ## 13) Suggested article primitives ### 13.1 Standard imports ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleResourceList from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Only include imports that are actually used. Do not import `ReactNode` unless you define a local type or prop that uses it. ### 13.2 FAQ data ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best way to start learning about {{TOPIC}}?', answer: 'Start with a practical problem, learn the minimum concepts needed to understand it, and build a small project you can explain clearly.', }, { question: 'Do I need a technical background?', answer: 'A technical background can help, but it is not the only path. Many people start by learning the language of the field, following practical examples, and joining communities where they can ask questions.', }, ] ``` ### 13.3 Audience grid ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Quote block ```tsx
